name: ExtensionCitation_Description
title: R5: Natural language description of the citation (new)
purpose:
This extension is part of the cross-version definitions generated to enable use of the
element `Citation.description` as defined in FHIR R5
in FHIR R4.

The source element is defined as:
`Citation.description` 0..1 `markdown`

Across FHIR versions, the element set has been mapped as:
*  R5: `Citation.description` 0..1 `markdown`

Following are the generation technical comments:
Element `Citation.description` is not mapped to FHIR R4, since FHIR R5 `Citation` is not mapped.
Element `Citation.description` has no mapping targets in FHIR R4. Typically, this is because the element has been added (is a new element).

element.short: R5: Natural language description of the citation (new)
element.comment:
Element `Citation.description` is not mapped to FHIR R4, since FHIR R5 `Citation` is not mapped.
Element `Citation.description` has no mapping targets in FHIR R4. Typically, this is because the element has been added (is a new element).
This description can be used to capture details such as comments about misuse, instructions for clinical use and interpretation, literature references, examples from the paper world, etc. It is not a rendering of the citation as conveyed in the 'text' field of the resource itself. This item SHOULD be populated unless the information is available from context (e.g. the language of the citation is presumed to be the predominant language in the place the citation was created).

element.short: Natural language description of the citation
element.definition: A free text natural language description of the citation from a consumer's perspective.
element.comment: This description can be used to capture details such as comments about misuse, instructions for clinical use and interpretation, literature references, examples from the paper world, etc. It is not a rendering of the citation as conveyed in the 'text' field of the resource itself. This item SHOULD be populated unless the information is available from context (e.g. the language of the citation is presumed to be the predominant language in the place the citation was created).
